title: Why Is My Cat Chewing on One Side? Dental Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat chewing on one side? Learn about dental pain, urgency levels, and why a photo of their mouth helps your vet provide the best care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Chewing on one side is a primary sign of dental pain or oral disease in cats and warrants a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours to address underlying discomfort.

What does it mean if my cat is only chewing on one side of their mouth?

When a cat favors one side of their mouth while eating, it is a primary clinical sign of oral discomfort or pain. This behavior, often called 'guarded chewing,' suggests that the cat is trying to avoid contact with a sensitive area. Common causes include dental resorptive lesions, fractured teeth, or localized gum disease. Because cats are evolutionarily driven to hide their pain, noticing this change in their eating habits is a significant observation of their well-being.

Is it an emergency if my cat is favoring one side of their mouth?

The urgency level for this issue is Medium. While chewing on one side is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it indicates that your cat is experiencing active pain every time they eat. To manage this condition, you should:

  • Aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Watch for a complete refusal to eat, which increases the level of urgency.
  • Monitor for accompanying signs such as significant facial swelling or bleeding from the mouth.

How does providing a photo of my cat's mouth help with veterinary triage?

Providing a photo of the inside of your cat's mouth can be extremely beneficial for a preliminary triage assessment. If your cat allows you to gently lift their lip, a clear image can help a professional:

  • Identify visible redness or gum inflammation.
  • Spot significant tartar buildup or broken tooth crowns.
  • Determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen by a doctor.
  • Prepare the necessary diagnostic tools before you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Unilateral chewing in cats can indicate dental disease. A thorough oral examination under general anesthesia is required for proper evaluation, as many intraoral lesions may be missed in a conscious animal. Dental radiographs are essential to assess for retained root fragments if extractions have been performed previously. Treatment often involves partial- or full-mouth extractions, along with debridement of affected soft and hard tissues, to provide lasting improvement. Any retained root fragments must be removed to prevent continued issues.

Chapter: Dentistry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 363)

faqs.question: Can I wait for my cat's annual exam to bring this up?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to wait. Dental pain can be intense and may lead to systemic infections or a complete loss of appetite if left untreated.

faqs.question: Are there other signs of dental pain I should look for?
faqs.answer: Yes, look for drooling, bad breath (halitosis), pawing at the face, or dropping food out of the mouth while eating.

faqs.question: Will changing to soft food fix the problem?
faqs.answer: Soft food may make it easier for your cat to swallow, but it will not treat the underlying cause of the pain. The dental issue will continue to progress without professional intervention.
